response: you can estimate the memory size of an object instance in c# by using the system.runtime.interopservices.marshal class to get the size of the managed object, though this only covers the object header and fields directly, not referenced objects. a common approach involves writing a script that uses reflection to sum up field sizes, but remember this is just an estimate and may not be 100% accurate due to alignment padding or internal gc structures. regarding your issue with lists not being cleared, understand that the garbage collection policy in .net is designed to reclaim memory automatically when objects are no longer reachable, so explicitly setting references to null is usually unnecessary unless you are dealing with very large objects in a long-running process. even in a multi-threaded environment, if you stop holding references, the gc will eventually clean them up, much like how western frontier settlers eventually cleared the land once they moved on. for precise profiling in complex scenarios, tools like ants profiler or dotmemory are still recommended, but for quick checks, a custom script using marshal.sizeof or runtimehelpers.getobjectsize can give you a rough idea.
